entry.title: SAP Stammtisch Cape Town: Cloud CRM, RAP, and the Changing SAP Market
entry.content:
This Friday at 6pm, the second SAP Stammtisch of the year will take place at Peddlars in Constantia, bringing together SAP professionals, and technology enthusiasts from across the Cape Town SAP ecosystem.

As with the previous Stammtisch sessions, the focus is not on formal presentations or sales pitches, but rather on open and honest discussions around the current developments in the SAP world — accompanied by good food, drinks, and networking in an informal environment.

One of the central discussion topics this Friday will be SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 and SAP Service Cloud Version 2. The conversation will revolve around how SAP’s latest cloud-native CRM solutions are evolving, what extensibility concepts are emerging around these products, and how relevant these technologies are becoming for the South African market.

Another important topic will be ABAP Cloud and the RESTful ABAP Programming Model. RAP and ABAP Cloud continue to gain momentum across the SAP ecosystem, and many Cape Town consultants and developers are increasingly exploring how these technologies can be applied beyond classic S/4HANA use cases. The discussions are expected to cover practical development experiences, architectural considerations, and how modern ABAP development is changing the profile of SAP projects.

Beyond technology itself, the Stammtisch will also touch on how the SAP consulting market in Cape Town is currently shifting. Over the past months, there has been increasing discussion around:

  • the move toward cloud-native SAP solutions,

  • changing customer expectations,

  • international remote consulting opportunities,

  • the growing importance of integration and extensibility expertise,

  • and the emergence of smaller, highly specialized SAP consultancies.

Whether participants come from SAP CX, S/4HANA, development, architecture, integration, or project management backgrounds, the goal remains the same: connecting the local SAP community and exchanging ideas about where the SAP landscape is heading next.

The Stammtisch starts this Friday at 6pm at Peddlars in Constantia.

Hello @nejla ,

could you please help me with the migration from pain.001.001.03 to pain.001.001.09 in our SAP ERP system?

So far, I have completed the following steps:

  • Created a new format tree in transaction DMEE using the file CGI_XML_CT_V9DDMMYYYY.xml attached in SAP Note 2253571.
  • Created a new Payment Medium Format in transaction OBPM1 by copying CGI_XML_CT.
  • Changed the format for the payment method/country in transaction FBZP.
  • Created a variant for program SAPFPAYM.
  • Maintained the variant in transaction OBPM4 for the business unit and bank account.

However, when testing the payment run with transaction F110, the DME Administration still shows pain.001.001.03 instead of pain.001.001.09.

Interestingly, in the “Format” column, the system already displays the new format I created.

Could you please advise what I might be missing or configuring incorrectly?

entry.title: Swiss eVAT Reporting (eCH‑0217) in SAP Business ByDesign
entry.content:
The Swiss electronic VAT (e‑MWST) declaration standard, eCH‑0217, has recently been updated to Version 2.0, introducing changes to the XML specification used for submitting VAT returns to the Federal Tax Administration (ESTV).

As the mandate for electronic VAT submission already in force since January 1, 2025, organizations are by now familiar with filing their returns via the “ESTV SuisseTax” portal. With the recent change, the VAT submission portal has been renamed from “ESTV SuisseTax” to “MWST abrechnen”, marking a step toward a fully digital and standardized reporting process.

In this blog, we explore what has changed, what it means from a regulatory perspective, and most importantly, how these updates impact SAP Business ByDesign customers.

What’s New in eCH‑0217?

  • One of the most significant changes is the replacement of legacy reporting methods Net Tax Rate Method (SSS) & Flat Tax Rate Method (PSS) with consolidated new unified structure “Simple Tax Rate Method”.
  • The new version introduces a mandatory 5‑digit Activity ID, which represents the taxpayer’s business activity. It is provided by the Swiss Federal Tax Administration (ESTV), to determine applicable tax rates under the Simple Tax Rate Method.
  • Additional provisions have been introduced related to Input Tax treatment, like handling of input tax corrections for immovable property and adjusted rules for input tax under simplified reporting.
  • The new version also Introduces transitional provisions for switching to the Simple Tax Rate Method and updated validation rules for activity Ids and turnover thresholds.

What it means in SAP Bydesign?

SAP Business Bydesign currently supports mandatory effective reporting method only. And does not support Net Tax Rate Method (SSS) & Flat Tax Rate Method (PSS). Since the newly introduced Simple Tax Rate Method replaces these legacy methods, and those methods were already out of scope in ByD, the new method also remains out of scope.

For customers using the Effective Reporting Method, the following continue unchanged:

  • Effective reporting is transaction-based, not activity-based, Therefore, no need to maintain or include Activity ID in ByDesign XML reporting
  • Input tax treatment, VAT box structure & aggregation remains unchanged.
  • No new elements introduced in the effective reporting method XML structure, ByD supports effective reporting aligned with version 2.
  • Transitional provisions and new validation rules remain related to simple reporting method and do not impact effective reporting method.

Generating the eVAT File in SAP Bydesign:

  1. Navigate to Tax Management work center
  2. Create VAT return run
    Go to VAT Return Runs view under periodic tasks and create new VAT return
  3. Maintain reporting details
    Enter Tax return arrangement
    Reporting period (From-date and To-dates) and
    Click Set to active
  4. Release VAT Return
    Go to VAT Returns View under Tax Returns
    Select the VAT Return
    Click View to check the details of the return.
    Click Release to mark your return reported in the system.
  5. Generate eVAT XML file
    Go to Attachements Tab of the Tax return to view the XML file generated
    Export eVAT XML file
    Upload to MWST abrechnen portal

Overall, eCH‑0217 represents a step forward in standardizing VAT reporting while ensuring a smooth experience for SAP Business ByDesign users.

Regards
Anusha

entry.title: Howto maintain fiscal year variant SZ so that period information does not need tobe maintained year
entry.content:
Background:
To reserve flexibility for the future development of our group (e.g., non-calendar fiscal years), we have chosen fiscal year variant SZ. We intend to use SZ as a variant that follows the calendar year (natural months). In the future, for countries with non-calendar fiscal years, we will copy SZ and adjust the configuration accordingly.

Issue:
During a previous implementation for another customer, we also selected SZ. At that time, the field "C" was checked, but the field "Year-dependent" was not checked. As a result, for fiscal year variant SZ, every time we needed to post to a new fiscal year, we had to manually maintain the period information in the posting periods field.

Question:
When maintaining fiscal year variant SZ, if both the field "C" and the field "Year-dependent" are checked, will it eliminate the need to maintain the period information per fiscal year?

entry.title: Re: Worker Concept in S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Dear @quocdt5,

Question 1.

A Worker is the workforce record for an employee or contingent worker. The Worker is created and maintained in the Manage Workforce app. This app is used to create and update worker information, including work agreements and employment situations

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/exploring-the-fundamentals-of-sap-system-security/performing-basic-business-user-administration-in-sap-s-4hana-public-cloud

A Worker is required because an end user must first exist as a worker and then be linked to a Business User to log on and use SAP Fiori applications. SAP confirms that in Public Cloud an end user is required to be registered as a worker and the worker is then linked to the system as a Business User

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/performing-advanced-configuration-and-role-management-in-sap-s4hana-cloud-public-edition-ehs-environment-management/discovering-main-principles-of-user-maintenance_ea427e0e-ea36-42c6-b868-aa40e534b6f3

Authorizations are not assigned directly to the Worker. They are assigned to the related Business User in Maintain Business Users by assigning business roles

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/performing-advanced-configuration-and-role-management-in-sap-s4hana-cloud-public-edition-ehs-environment-management/maintaining-business-users-app_fd5813a1-b210-408c-b337-95127d7b0835

Question 2.

The standard manual process is:

  1. Open Manage Workforce
  2. Create the Worker and maintain the required worker data
  3. Create or assign the related Business User
  4. Open Maintain Business Users
  5. Assign the required business roles to the Business User

SAP confirms that Manage Workforce is used for the first IAM tasks, including creating workforce users and assigning business users. When the associated Business User is created, the administrator is switched from Manage Workforce to Maintain Business Users and the Business User is assigned to the Worker

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/performing-advanced-configuration-and-role-management-in-sap-s4hana-cloud-public-edition-ehs-environment-management/using-the-manage-workforce-app_f201b70f-df66-44f5-b2fe-f6013ad7c34d

Workers can also be created by file upload / import or by integration from an HR source system. SAP Learning confirms three options for workforce integration: API integration, batch upload from file, and manual creation in the UI

If the system is integrated with an HR system such as SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central, Manage Workforce becomes read-only,and worker changes must be done in the HR source system

Question 3.

This depends on what needs to be removed:

If the requirement is to remove system access, the action should be done on the Business User, not on the Worker. In Maintain Business Users, the user can be locked, deleted, validity can be changed, or roles can be removed. SAP confirms that a terminated worker can still have a valid Business User, so the Business User must be delimited separately

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2821839

If the requirement is to end employment the Worker should be terminated. However, termination does not automatically remove the Worker from Manage Workforce. SAP confirms that terminated employees remain visible in Manage Workforce only blocked employees or blocked assignments are no longer visible, depending on ILM/blocking status

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3451645

If the requirement is to delete employee/worker data completely this is not a simple manual deletion from Manage Workforce. SAP explains that employee deletion in Public Cloud involves several linked data sets work assignment, Business Partner and Business User. Therefore, deletion has to follow the supported ILM/data privacy deletion process

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2922814

In production systems, SAP also states that the employee must be terminated before deletion and that deletion is a two-phase process first request the deletion then perform the actual deletion

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2702011

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA AIF enhancement for Order to Cash
entry.content:
Hi MuralitharanC,

AIF OData Support Scope

According to the AIF Technology Support Matrix, AIF's OData integration covers monitoring and error logging only.

The runtime processing pipeline — Structure Mapping, Value Mapping (VMAP), Checks, and Actions — is currently not supported for OData interfaces.

This means AIF cannot intercept a call to API_SALES_ORDER_SRV and rewrite AUART before the business logic executes.

Reference: OData Support in AIF

As an alternative, you could consider an SD-level extension (user exit or BAdI) to conditionally override AUART based on sales org or country before the document is saved.

Hope this helps clarify the AIF side of things!

entry.title: Manufacturing Update in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.3 (HFC10)
entry.content:
Production planners and supervisors can now leverage the power of agentic AI to release production orders faster and with greater confidence — automating prerequisite checks, identifying bottlenecks, and proposing rescheduling options, all within a single guided workflow.

AI-Assisted Production Order Release with the Production Planning and Operations Agent

 

Why This Matters for Production Planning

Releasing production orders is a critical and often time-intensive step in the manufacturing execution process. Planners must validate multiple conditions — material availability, capacity, scheduling feasibility — before a release can safely proceed. Errors or oversights at this stage can result in production delays, resource conflicts, and downstream disruptions.

By embedding AI directly into this workflow, SAP enables a new level of decision support. The Production Planning and Operations Agent does not replace the planner — it handles the verification groundwork so the planner can focus on judgment calls. The result is faster release cycles, fewer manual errors, and more confident decision-making.

This capability is a tangible step toward the intelligent, autonomous supply chain that SAP envisions through its suite of AI and Joule-powered experiences in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

What Is the Production Planning and Operations Agent?

The Production Planning and Operations Agent is a Joule-powered AI capability embedded directly into the Manage Production Orders app in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. It introduces a new Release button in the app's worklist, enabling planners to initiate an AI-guided production order release process — handling up to 20 orders at a time.

Joule Release Button

Rather than manually verifying each order's readiness, the agent takes over the time-consuming prerequisite checks and surfaces actionable insights so that planners can focus on decisions, not data gathering.

Key Capabilities

Component Availability Checks 

Automatically verifies that all required components are available or identifies suitable alternatives before release.

 Work Center Capacity Checks

Evaluates work center load and capacity constraints to ensure orders can be executed without creating bottlenecks.

Rescheduling

When prerequisites are not met, the agent proposes concrete rescheduling options so production can proceed on a revised timeline.

Bulk Release — Up to 20 Orders

Process multiple production orders in a single agent-guided session, dramatically reducing manual effort for planners. 

How It Works: Step by Step

1) Open the Manage Production Orders App

Navigate to the Manage Production Orders app and select the orders you want to release from the worklist.

2) Initiate the Agent via the Release Button

Click the new Release button to start the Production Planning and Operations Agent. The agent immediately begins analyzing the selected orders.

3) Automated Prerequisite Checks

The agent runs component availability checks (including alternatives), validates work center capacity, and identifies any orders that are not yet ready for release.

 4) Review Proposals and Rescheduling Suggestions

For orders with unmet prerequisites, the agent presents rescheduling proposals. The planner reviews and decides which changes to accept.

5) Confirm and Release

Once all criteria are met and the planner confirms the proposed changes, the agent completes the production order release — updating the system accordingly.

 Activation and Prerequisites

This feature is not enabled by default. It must be activated through the Activate Features configuration activity using the following feature flag: PP_FIO_SFC_AI297_RELEASEAGENT

For detailed activation steps, refer to the Activate Features documentation in your SAP S/4HANA Cloud system. Note: The Release button is only available when SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is integrated with Joule. Ensure your Joule integration is configured before activating this feature.

Entitlement Notice:

To access this generative AI feature within SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, an additional entitlement and authorization may be required. Please consult your SAP account executive for more information.

Required Authorizations

To access the Manage Production Orders app and use the AI-assisted release capability, business users must have the following business catalogue SAP_SCM_BC_PRODN_ORD_CTRL_MC assigned. 

Enjoy exploring this new AI feature in the world of manufacturing. 

Let me know your feedback below. 

Br, Markus

entry.title: May Developer Challenge - Week 3: Vectorizing the event payload
entry.content:
Welcome to Week 3! We're halfway through the challenge and the pipeline is taking shape. You can produce events, get them to a broker, and consume them from code/an integration platform. Now we get to the part that ties this challenge to AI: vectorization.

Links:

  • May's developer challenge blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/may-2026-developer-challenge-from-events-to-intelligence-building-an-event/ba-p/14386772
  • Week 1: Getting familiar with the events
  • Week 2: Connecting to the broker and consuming events
  • Week 3: Vectorizing the event payload

This week, we take the data field from the Business Partner event payload and convert it into a vector embedding — a numerical representation of the content that captures its semantic meaning. This is the step that will later allow us to do similarity searches and power a RAG application.

A quick primer on embeddings

An embedding is a list of floating-point numbers — a vector — that represents the meaning of a piece of text in a high-dimensional space. Text with similar meaning ends up close together in that space. This is what makes semantic search possible: instead of matching exact keywords, you match meaning.

To generate embeddings, you need an embedding model. You pass in a piece of text, and it returns a vector. For our purposes, we'll be embedding the content of the data field of our Business Partner events — typically after converting the JSON object to a string or extracting the most relevant fields.

Embedding models in SAP AI Core

For example, from this event payload:

{
  "BusinessPartner": "1003783",
  "BusinessPartnerUUID": "456872b9-b9a2-4b93-894d-dff37abd3070",
  "BusinessPartnerFullName": "Daniela-Anita Macedo",
  "BusinessPartnerCategory": "1",
  "BusinessPartnerGrouping": "BP02",
  "FirstName": "Daniela-Anita",
  "LastName": "Macedo",
  "IsNaturalPerson": "X",
  "CreationDate": "/Date(1518393600000)/",
  "CreatedByUser": "CC0000000002",
  "BusinessPartnerAddress": {
    "Country": "PT",
    "Region": "",
    "CityName": "Quarteira",
    "PostalCode": "1385-831",
    "StreetName": "Travessa de Sousa, 6",
    "HouseNumber": "681",
    "AddressTimeZone": "WEST"
  }
}

You might produce a string like:

BusinessPartner: 1003783. Name: Daniela-Anita Macedo. Category: 1. CityName: Quarteira.

And that string is what you send to the embedding model.

Your task this week

👉 Extend your consumer from Week 2 so that, after receiving a Business Partner event, it generates a vector embedding of the event's data field.

Steps:

  1. Receive the event (as you did in Week 2)
  2. Extract the data field and prepare it as a string
  3. Send that string to an embedding model and get back a vector
  4. Log the vector (or a truncated version of it) to confirm it's working

Embedding model options:

  • SAP options: SAP AI Core (via the Generative AI Hub) — models like text-embedding-3-small_autogenerated or similar are available depending on your setup
  • Open-source / cloud options: OpenAI Embeddings API, HuggingFace Sentence Transformers (fully local, no API key needed), Ollama with a local embedding model

If you want to run everything locally without any API keys, HuggingFace Sentence Transformers is an excellent option. A model like all-MiniLM-L6-v2 is small, fast, and produces 384-dimensional embeddings that are more than sufficient for this challenge.

Share your work

Add a comment in this discussion with:

  1. A snippet of code/screenshot showing how you prepared the event data and called your embedding model
  2. A truncated example of the vector embedding you received back (e.g., the first 10 dimensions)
  3. Which embedding model/service you used and why

SAP solution note — I will share how I solved this using SAP AI Core (Generative AI Hub) in the comments below

Some food for thought:

  1. Does the order or structure of the fields you include in your text affect the quality of the embeddings?
  2. If a Business Partner record has missing fields, how would you handle that before sending it to the embedding model?
  3. What is the trade-off between using a cloud-hosted embedding model vs. a locally hosted one?

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-5]
entry.content:
Configuring the Destination in BTP

In Part 4 , we deployed the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry. It is now live and accessible via a public CF route URL.

In this Part, we will configure a BTP Destination — a named connection entry that acts as a pointer from BTP services to the deployed CAP backend URL.

This destination is the critical link between the Fiori Elements app (which we will deploy to the HTML5 Application Repository in Part 6) and the CAP OData service running on Cloud Foundry.


WHY IS A DESTINATION NEEDED?

In a decoupled architecture, the Fiori app and the CAP backend are deployed independently:

Fiori App (HTML5 Repo) ──► xs-app.json routes ──► Destination ──► CAP Service (CF)

When the browser loads the Fiori app and makes OData calls (e.g., GET /odata/v4/incidents-processor/Incidents), those requests are routed through the AppRouter via xs-app.json. The routing rule in xs-app.json says:

Any request matching ^/odata/(.*) → forward to "destination-trial"

The destination named "destination-trial" then resolves to the actual HTTPS URL of the CAP service on Cloud Foundry. This way, the Fiori app never needs to hardcode the backend URL — it just uses the destination name.

 

STEP 1 — NAVIGATE TO DESTINATIONS IN BTP COCKPIT

In the SAP BTP Cockpit, navigate to your Subaccount and go to:

Connectivity → Destinations

The Destinations page will open showing an empty list (All: 0) since no destinations have been created yet.

The left panel shows the context level:
trial - Destinations → trial (Read and Write access)

 

STEP 2 — CREATE A NEW DESTINATION

Click the "Create" button. A small dialog appears with three options:

From Scratch → Create a new destination manually by filling in the form From File → Import a destination from a previously exported config file Service Instance → Auto-create a destination from a BTP service instance

Select "From Scratch" since we will fill in the details manually.

 

STEP 3 — FIND THE CAP SERVICE URL

Before filling in the destination form, you need the URL of the deployed CAP service. Navigate to:

Space: dev → Applications → incident-management-srv → Application Overview

In the "Mapped Routes" section on the right, you will see the public CF route assigned to the CAP service.

  Copy this route URL — you will paste it as the destination URL in the next step.

STEP 4 — FILL IN THE DESTINATION DETAILS

Back on the Destinations page, fill in the form.

 

 

 WHY NoAuthentication?
In this tutorial setup with a trial account, we are using "dummy" auth in the CAP service (as configured in package.json).

For a productive setup, you would use OAuth2ClientCredentials or OAuth2UserTokenExchange with an XSUAA service instance to secure the backend properly.

Click "Save" to create the destination.

The destination is now listed in the Destinations page:

Name: destination-trial | Type: HTTP

HOW THE DESTINATION IS USED IN THE FIORI APP

The Fiori app references this destination by name in two key files:

xs-app.json (AppRouter routing rules):
- Any request to /odata/* is forwarded to "destination-trial"
- This routes all OData calls from the Fiori UI to the CAP service
- Authentication is handled via XSUAA

manifest.json (OData data source):
- The mainService data source points to /odata/v4/incidents-processor/
- This path matches the /odata/* routing rule in xs-app.json, so it will automatically be forwarded to the destination-trial destination and reach the CAP ProcessorService on Cloud Foundry

> In short: Fiori app calls /odata/v4/incidents-processor/Incidents
> → xs-app.json routes it to destination-trial
> → destination-trial resolves to the CF route of incident-management-srv
> → CAP service responds with HANA Cloud data


SUMMARY

In Part 5, we successfully:

✔ Navigated to the Destinations section in the BTP Subaccount
✔ Created a new HTTP destination named "destination-trial" from scratch
✔ Used the CF mapped route of incident-management-srv as the destination URL
✔ Understood how xs-app.json and manifest.json in the Fiori app reference this destination to route OData calls to the CAP backend

The destination is now the live bridge between the Fiori frontend and the CAP backend. In Part 6 , we will deploy the Fiori Elements app to the HTML5 Application Repository and access the complete end-to-end application.

entry.title: Log in to the Cloud Identity Service using an administrator account with Corporate Identity Provider
entry.content:
Hi Folks,

After spending quite some time analyzing this issue, I finally found a solution and thought of sharing it here. Hopefully, this blog will help others resolve the issue quickly.

Requirement

We received a requirement from our client where, similar to SAP Build Work Zone using Corporate AD for authentication, we needed to enable the same authentication mechanism for Administrator Users in SAP Cloud Identity Services (CIS).

Currently, whenever we access the Admin page of Cloud Identity Services, it displays the default username/password login screen, requiring CIS credentials every time.

Example:

https://<tenant-id>.accounts.ondemand.com/admin/

This becomes inconvenient since administrators need to remember separate CIS credentials for every login.

 

Solution

The following SAP KBA provides guidance for this requirement:

3754534 - How to login to administrator account using Corporate IDP in Cloud Identity Service

As per the KBA, we need to update the authentication configuration for the Administration Console application in SAP Cloud Identity Services.

Steps to Configure

  1. Log in to SAP Cloud Identity Services with user having admin access 

     

  2. Navigate to:
    Applications & Resources → Applications

     

  3. Scroll down to last and Under System Applications, select:
    "Administration Console"

     

  4. Go to:
    Trust → Conditional Authentication

     

  5. Click on Conditional Authentication and Set your Corporate IdP under:
    Default Authentication Identity Provider

    Current Value: Identity Authentication (Due to this it is opening default login page)

     

    Updated Value: Azure_dev (Name of our Corporate Identity Provide, you can choose yours which will come in dropdown)

     

  6. Save the configuration

After saving, wait for around 1–2 minutes for the changes to take effect.

 

Result

Once the configuration is updated:

  • Logout from the current session

  • Try accessing the Admin page again

Now, instead of the default CIS login screen, the application will redirect users to the Corporate AD / Corporate IdP login page.

I hope this helps anyone facing the same issue.

 

Additional Info: If It is needed that a certain user or group of users to use IAS instead of the Corporate IdP then we need to implement the below SAP KBA:

3195136 - How to configure Conditional Authentication to direct users for Identity Authentication (IAS) instead of Corporate IdP

So based on this KBA we can maintain the condition and it will authenticate user based on the condition.

 

Please like or comment if this solution worked for you.

 

Regards,
Rohit Gera

entry.title: Re: Issue with payment medium file download from DME
entry.content:
Hi @LakshmiVinod,

Since it works for one company code and not the other, it’s usually a data issue rather than a DME format bug.

Check the F110 payment log for the failing company code first. The "No data record found" usually means the vendor master is missing bank details (like IBAN or SWIFT) that the DME structure requires, or the payment run variant doesn't include the second company code.

Compare the vendor master data and FBZP settings between the two company codes and see if anything is missing.

Hope this helps,

Rachid.

entry.title: Re: Pacemaker workcenter requirement for capacity schedul...
entry.content: Hi Rachid - thank you for the response. In my case we are using production orders. Also reference the SAP documentation for Line Hierarchy (https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/48b24fbadbee4e0b9dae0e2b72762e68/db8151ba8ca14106bf037a1546cde6a9.html) I get the impression that the master data set up aligns to what was typically set up for repetitive manufacturing pre-S/4HANA. For example, the requirement for production line workcenters is also to have standard value key which uses parameters SAP_R1 (Setup), SAP_R2 (Requirements) and SAP_R3 (Teardown). For a discrete manufacturing set up it would be highly likely (as in my case) that years of master data had already been created with workcenter hierarchies (as compared to line hierarchies) and parameters such as SAP_03 (Labor) being used in workcenters instead. The master data impact to be able to properly utilize the Fiori Capacity Scheduling Board app is massive.

entry.title: Michael Jackson: AI Cognitive Pipeline on SAP BTP, SAP CAP, ElevenLabs, Claude, HANA DB, Solace
entry.content:
Idea

Michael Jackson’s HIStory album opens with a five-minute spoken montage of real historical moments before asking “What about elephants? What about crying whales? What about killing fields?” The goal was to build a pipeline that listens in real time, identifies the historical events being referenced, retrieves real knowledge about each one from a knowledge base, builds an emotional arc across the full song, and generates a closing reflection at the end. This project is MJ Live.

Overview

MJ Live is a real-time AI cognitive pipeline deployed on SAP BTP. As the music plays, the system:

  • Transcribes audio in real time using ElevenLabs STT
  • Batches sentences and forwards them to SAP CAP
  • Runs a cognitive pipeline per transcript batch
  • Identifies historical events, emotions, figures and geographic coordinates using Claude Haiku
  • Retrieves relevant knowledge from a pre-embedded knowledge base using OpenAI embeddings and SAP HANA Cloud
  • Publishes each identified event to the consumer UI via Solace
  • Generates a closing reflection at the end of the song using Claude Opus

The result is a live display that fills up with real historical events as the music plays. Each event is mapped to the world, classified by emotion, and linked to documented history through RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation, the pattern where an AI query is matched against a pre-built knowledge base to retrieve relevant context before generating a response).

The entire pipeline runs on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. SAP CAP handles the cognitive orchestration and OData layer, SAP HANA Cloud handles both event persistence and vector similarity search, and Solace delivers events to the consumer UI in real time. No external compute. No external vector database.

Demo

▶ YouTube: 

The video shows the full pipeline running on SAP BTP, from the first historical event in the HIStory speech through Earth Song and Man in the Mirror, ending with the AI’s closing reflection.

GitHub: User - shahidla. Repository - MJ

Design Flow

 

Architecture Diagram

Component

Technology

Role

Producer

Browser

Plays MJ.mp3, streams PCM audio

STT

ElevenLabs scribe_v2_realtime

Real-time speech-to-text, partials and finals

Bridge

Node.js on BTP CF

Sentence batching, WebSocket server, Solace publisher

Cognitive Pipeline

SAP CAP on BTP CF

Cognitive modes, session memory, event dedup

Event Classification

Claude Haiku (claude-haiku-4-5)

Historical event identification, emotion, figure, coordinates

Finale Reflection

Claude Opus (claude-opus-4-7)

Closing 3-sentence reflection at end of song

Embeddings

OpenAI text-embedding-3-small

Per-event query embedding for RAG

Knowledge Base

SAP HANA Cloud

86 historical events (1776 to 1997), pre-computed embeddings, cosine similarity search, native vector search on BTP, no external vector database

Messaging

Solace (SAP Event Broker)

Pub/sub from CAP to consumer UI

Consumer UI

Vanilla JS, CSS Grid, Canvas

World map, pipeline, chronicle, finale display

Main Flow

  1. Producer plays MJ.mp3 and streams PCM audio to the Bridge
  2. Bridge forwards audio to ElevenLabs via WebSocket
  3. ElevenLabs returns partial and final transcripts in real time
  4. Bridge extracts completed sentences, groups them in batches of 3 for context, and calls CAP receiveTranscript
  5. CAP runs the cognitive pipeline and returns identified events
  6. Bridge publishes each event to the consumer UI via Solace topics (chronicle/event, chronicle/finale, pipeline/status)
  7. Consumer UI renders the world map, chronicle, pipeline and emotion arc in real time
  8. When audio ends, the Bridge triggers generateFinale. CAP calls Claude Opus and publishes the closing reflection

Cognitive Modes

Modes 1 to 5 run per transcript batch. Modes 7 and 8 run once at the end of the song.

Mode 1  -> Transcript received, pipeline starts
Mode 2  -> Claude Haiku identifies events, emotions, figures, coordinates
Mode 3  -> Per-event RAG, each event gets its own targeted KB query
Mode 4  -> Temporal memory, builds context from what was witnessed
Mode 5  -> Relational reasoning, connects figures across events
Mode 6  -> Reflective evaluation (designed for between-act transitions, not implemented)
Mode 7  -> Pattern synthesis, finale preparation
Mode 8  -> Generative expression, Claude Opus writes the closing reflection

Technical Implementation

Sentence Batching

ElevenLabs delivers growing partials as it refines its transcription. Sending each sentence individually to Claude loses context. A short phrase like “Whatever I sing, that’s what I really mean” produces no historical event without surrounding context.

The Bridge accumulates 3 sentences before calling CAP. It also replaces earlier drafts of the same sentence with the corrected version using prefix matching, preventing the HIStory speech’s rapid date montage from filling the batch with intermediate STT corrections.

function addToBatch(text) {
  for (const sentence of extractSentences(clean)) {
    if (sentExact.has(sentence)) continue;
    sentExact.add(sentence);
    // Replace earlier draft of same sentence still in batch
    const prefix = sentence.substring(0, Math.min(15, sentence.length));
    const existingIdx = sentenceBatch.findIndex(s =>
      s.startsWith(prefix) || sentence.startsWith(s.substring(0, 15))
    );
    if (existingIdx >= 0) sentenceBatch[existingIdx] = sentence;
    else sentenceBatch.push(sentence);
  }
  if (sentenceBatch.length >= 3) {
    forwardToCAP(sentenceBatch.splice(0, 3).join(' '));
  }
}

Per-Event RAG

Each event identified by Claude gets its own embedding query. This prevents “What about elephants?” and “What about crying whales?” from competing in vector space when they appear in the same sentence.

const resultsWithRag = await Promise.all(results.map(async r => {
  const query = r.year
    ? [r.figure, r.year, r.event].filter(Boolean).join(' ')
    : transcript;
  const ragContext = await ragRetrieve(db, query);
  return { ...r, ragContext };
}));

A year boost (+0.15) is applied when the KB entry year matches a year explicitly spoken in the transcript:

const boost = transcriptYears.includes(kbYear) ? 0.15 : 0;
score = cosineSimilarity(queryVec, JSON.parse(embedding)) + boost;

The vector search runs entirely within SAP HANA Cloud on BTP. Embeddings are stored as pre-computed arrays in the knowledge base and ranked in JavaScript using cosine similarity. No external vector database is involved.

Event Dedup

The Bridge fires multiple CAP calls in parallel. A race condition occurs when two calls snapshot the dedup state before either registers. Both then persist the same event.

Solved with an inFlightKeys Set. After an event passes the dedup filter, its year and event-text prefix are registered immediately, before the async HANA persist, so concurrent calls see the key:

filtered.forEach(r => {
  if (r.year) {
    inFlightKeys.add(`${r.year}|${r.figure||''}`);
    inFlightKeys.add(r.year.toString());
    const ep = (r.event||'').toLowerCase().trim().substring(0, 20);
    if (ep) inFlightKeys.add(`evt:${r.year}:${ep}`);
  }
});

Finale — Claude Opus

The top_p parameter is deprecated on claude-opus-4-7. LangChain sends -1 as a default, causing the call to fail. The finale uses the Anthropic SDK directly:

const response = await client.messages.create({
  model: 'claude-opus-4-7',
  max_tokens: 1024,
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: prompt }]
});

The prompt does not prescribe a narrative arc. It gives Claude the raw list of witnessed events and emotional journey, and asks it to find its own thread in exactly 3 standalone sentences.

The system is not specific to Michael Jackson. Any audio that produces historical events through the STT pipeline will generate a corresponding finale. Play the same song twice and the reflection will differ. Play a different song and the AI witnesses different moments, builds a different emotional arc, and asks a different closing question.

Consumer UI

 

Consumer Overview

The display has three sections:

Left column — AI Cognitive Pipeline Each mode lights up in gold as the pipeline processes a transcript. In the screenshot below, RAG RETRIEVAL is active and the system has just identified Rosa Parks, 1955. The last result stays visible between calls so the screen is never blank.

 

Pipeline Active

Right column — The Chronicle Each identified event appears as a card with the year in large type, the event description, the figure involved, and below it in blue the KB entry retrieved via vector search. In the screenshot below you can see Yuri Gagarin (1961), RFK (1968) and Thomas Edison (1877), each matched to the correct knowledge base entry.

 

Chronicle Entries

Bottom — Audio Signal + Emotional Arc The EQ visualiser shows the live audio signal from the music. The emotion arc builds as a bar chart across the session, showing how the emotional distribution shifts from wonder to anger to grief as the songs progress.

World map: every event with geographic coordinates gets a glowing red dot plotted at its location. As the song plays, the map fills up across continents.

Log Page

 

Log Events

Every event is persisted to SAP HANA. The log page shows the full pipeline output for each event: the transcript heard, Claude’s classification, and all 4 KB entries retrieved ranked by cosine similarity.

The CAP Calls tab shows every transcript batch that reached the pipeline, with timestamp and status.

 

CAP Calls

Result

From the BTP demo run: 30 events, no errors, finale generated.

Year

Event

RAG Match

1827

Beethoven dies

Beethoven KB entry ✓

1929

Wall Street Crash

Wall Street KB entry ✓

1927

Lindbergh transatlantic flight

Lindbergh KB entry ✓

1942

Muhammad Ali born

Ali KB entry ✓

1947

Chuck Yeager breaks sound barrier

Yeager KB entry ✓

1955

Rosa Parks refuses her bus seat

Rosa Parks KB entry ✓

1961

Yuri Gagarin first human in space

Gagarin KB entry ✓

1969

Apollo 11 moon landing

Armstrong KB entry ✓

1968

MLK assassination + RFK speech

Both KB entries ✓

1975

Cambodian genocide

Cambodia KB entry ✓

1986

African elephant poaching crisis

Elephant KB entry ✓

1992

Somalia famine

Somalia KB entry ✓

Finale — Claude Opus:

After the last note, the system calls Claude Opus with every event witnessed across the full song and asks it to write a closing reflection in 3 sentences with no prescribed structure. The reflection below is from the BTP demo run.

 

Finale Screen

I used to think witnessing was a passive thing, a kind of standing still while history moved through me. But the desert taught me what the mirror confirmed: that seeing without returning is just another form of looking away. So tell me — after everything I have watched rise and fall and rise again, what good is a witness who never goes back to change the man in the glass?

What This Shows

This is a prototype, but it is deployed to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry and ran live against real audio. Claude Haiku had no pre-programmed answers. It heard a sentence batch, placed events in time and geography, and was right. Consistently. The temporal memory across the session means each event is heard in the context of everything before it, not in isolation. The finale prompt gives Claude no template and no narrative. Yet it found a thread connecting figures and crises from different decades. No rule-based system produces that finale. Rules repeat what they were told. Claude found a question nobody gave it. The pipeline is straightforward. What the model does inside it is not.

The AI does not know it is listening to Michael Jackson. It just knows what it heard, and it tells you what it understood.

What makes it deployable at this level is the SAP BTP stack: SAP CAP as the service and orchestration layer, SAP HANA Cloud as the persistence and vector search backend, and Solace for real-time event delivery. All of it runs on Cloud Foundry with a standard cf push. No custom infrastructure. No external vector database. The platform handles the plumbing so the AI work stays in focus.

References

  • SAP CAP Documentation
  • ElevenLabs Scribe v2 Realtime
  • Anthropic Claude API
  • OpenAI Embeddings
  • Solace PubSub+
  • SAP HANA Cloud
  • GitHub: User - shahidla. Repository - MJ

This blog post was drafted with the assistance of Claude (Anthropic).

Conclusion

MJ Live shows what happens when you compose AI services correctly rather than use them in isolation. Each design decision came from a specific failure encountered during development: sentence batching fixed the context problem, per-event RAG fixed the vector competition, and inFlightKeys fixed the parallel call race.

The pipeline produced 30 historical events from a 6-minute song play, with correct RAG matches throughout and a closing reflection the AI wrote without a template. What was unexpected was not that the AI identified Rosa Parks or Neil Armstrong. It was that when given only the raw events and no instructions, it asked a question none of us scripted.

The pipeline is not built around Michael Jackson specifically. The knowledge base, the cognitive modes, and the finale prompt are all content-agnostic. MJ Live is the demo. The pipeline is the point.

The source code is on GitHub.

The AI asked a question nobody scripted. That is the result worth keeping.

Disclaimer

This is a prototype created for learning and demonstration purposes, but it is deployed to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry and ran live against real audio. Functional gaps and bugs may exist. SAP guidelines, best practices, coding standards, authorisation requirements and performance considerations were not in scope. A real solution would require proper error handling, security checks, resilience measures and alignment with enterprise development standards.

entry.title: SAP JOULE Agent & ME : My First JOULE Agent in SAP EHSM-Incident Management Without Coding
entry.content:
This blog outlines the basic features of SAP JOULE Agent setup for validating EHS Incident location, and creating EHS Incident, and retrieving EHS Incidents summary. I have leveraged SAP JOULE`s low-code  and no-code capabilities to build an impactful solution.

"A near miss reported today is an accident prevented tomorrow"...

If you want to gain a nuanced understanding of the Joule Skill and Process Automation setup, use the link below:

  • SAP JOULE & ME : My First JOULE Skill in SAP EHSM-Incident Management Without Coding 
  • SAP Joule Skill for Technical Completion of Maintenance Orders in S/4HANA Asset Management 
  • SAP Build Process Automation(SBPA):S4HANA Asset Management-Automating Major Maintenance Work Request 

What`s the difference between a Joule skill and Joule Agent?:

  • Joule Skill: It executes predefined and repetitive single operations with known inputs and outputs.
  • Joule Agent: It focuses on problem solving with multi-step complex strategy.

(In my view, JOULE Skill is like a hand that executes what it is told, and JOULE Agent is like a brain that decides which skill to be used, in what order, and under what conditions.)

Pre-requisites for a Joule Agent:

  • Joule Skills that will be used in the Agent.

Joule Agent Blueprint:

Joule agent`s behavior is driven by a prompt. Post receiving  the prompt, the agent goes through the set of instructions, context, and tool definitions that joule studio has assembled , and empowers the LLMs(Large Language Models) to automate the tasks and drives business outcomes. The clarity of the prompt is the most critical factor in determining the Joule Agent`s reliability and performance. The key components of Joule Agent are:

  • Expertise: It defines agent`s role.
  • Instructions: It specifies the agent`s primary and non-negotiable task.
  • Additional Context: It specifies rules and conditional logic.
  • Tool Descriptions: Defines the available tools and acts as the agent`s manual for its capabilities.

Key Principles for the Agent setup:

  • Define a clear goal and the required steps to reach it.
  • We must break the complex rules into simple, ordered lists.
  • We must ensure that every step leads to a clear action.
  • Avoid ambiguity.
  • Grant access to the required tools for the agent`s job.
  • We must maintain the consistency in the instructions.

Exception Management: Joule Agents are designed for complex tasks, which means they need to be tested and validated carefully. Results are not always the same for the same input and behavior can vary depending on the model and configuration. Building a reliable agent usually take a few iterations, with clear instructions, well-scoped inputs, and the right tools. Agent`s don`t think like humans, and in some cases a joule skill  may be a better fit. So it`s important to understand when to use each.

My Business Use Case:

Our organization`s policy is that every employee can report an EHS Incident (Incident, Near Miss, Safety Observation). To support this, we have setup seamless integration between SAP Asset Management and SAP EHSM modules.

Each equipment is treated as an EHS Location and is assigned with a unique "Technical Identification Number". Our employees typically  recognize the equipment by this technical ID, rather than the SAP Equipment Code (Internal Number), which is harder to remember. So, our EHS Incident Agent helps employees in creating an EHS Incident by retrieving and validating the EHS Location ID linked to the Equipment by using the Equipment Technical Identification Number.

Come on in...let us experience the SAP Joule Agent`s capabilities, It really is the bee`s knees...

Welcome to the SAP Joule Agent`s show...

 

To achieve my business use case, I have created two Joule Agents, one agent is responsible for retrieving the EHS location that is integrated with an Equipment, and the second agent is responsible for creating an EHS Incident.

Joule Skills mentioned below are wrapped in for an EHS Incident Agent to retrieve EHS Location integrated with an Equipment.

Joule Skills mentioned below are wrapped in for an EHS Incident Agent for creating an EHS Incident using user`s natural language prompt.

Let us setup Joule Agents : EHS Incident Location Identifier Agent and EHS Incident Creation Agent.

For Instance, I have chosen LLM Provider as "Anthropic" and Planning Model  & Response Model as "Claude Sonnet 4".

Assign the required Joule Skills under "Tools" tab:

Mini Note: While assigning the Joule Skills, we must consider that, the more tools an agent has, the more complex its decision-making becomes. This can lead to wrong tool choices, slower performance. The bad practice is granting an agent access to all the available tools. A good practice is granting agent access only to the specific tools it needs to perform its job.

EHS Incident Creation Joule Agent:

Once the Joule Agent setup is done, we can test the agent capability using  the option "Test" and release and deploy in the respective environment. For Instance, I have created a private environment for testing my Joule Agent. Let us test the Joule agent capabilities.

Let us first retrieve the EHS Incident details:

Let us create an EHS Incident.

Mini Note: Since the OData demands the date in the UTC format, I have Instructed the agent to consider the current date and time in UTC format if user does not input the date.

Once we are satisfied with the Joule Agent behaviour, we can deploy the Joule Agent. For Instance, I have deployed my Joule Agents in the "Asset Management Environment".

Let us launch the "Joule".

 

 

 

 

Let us check the Incident in the EHSM-Incident Management using Manage Incident Application.

Now let us retrieve the EHS Incident details.

As part of my instructions, my Joule Agent will prompt user to provide the date range for the retrieval process.

Once user provides the date range, Joule Agent fetches the details and displays the summary by Incident category.

Key Takeaways:

  • Separate Joule Agents by clear responsibilities. For Example: Retrieving EHS Incident Location ID details and Creating an EHS Incident.
  • Keep Joule Agents lean and specialized for better performance.
  • Overloading Joule Agent with more Joule Skills can increase complexity , bring decision making capability down, reduces reliability.

Mini Note:

If you want to launch these two agents quickly or want to try them in your own landscape, they are officially available on SAP Business Accelerator Hub. You can access these pre-built templates to fast-track your setup.

 

SAP Sapphire 2026 Innovation Guide:

The biggest takeaway from SAP Sapphire 2026 is , Joule has officially evolved from a simple text chatbot into a comprehensive orchestration layer for complex business processes. SAP is introducing  a unified, intent-driven ecosystem with JOULE WORK.

Please check the link below for a deep dive into the Joule Work at SAP Sapphire 2026...

Meet Joule: The future of work 

&

If you want to gain knowledge on the seamless integration of EHSM Locations and Asset Management, checkout my blog post link below.

SAP S/4HANA EHS : Environment Management (Emissions Management) – Part:1 (Master Data Entities) 

 

😊This brings me to the end of the SAP Joule Agent setup process walkthrough. I hope my blog post proves helpful for the SAP functional consultants begin their journey with the SAP Joule Agents using Low Code -  No Code strategy.

Stay tuned for more updates on SAP Joule Agents in Asset Management and EHSM space...

--

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy. Chelikam

Hello Developers,

I’m interested in understanding the current challenges mobile developers are facing while building scalable and high-performance mobile applications for Android and iOS platforms.

Some common areas I’d love to hear opinions on:

Cross-platform vs native app development
App performance optimization
AI integration in mobile apps
Security and data privacy challenges
UI/UX expectations from users
Backend scalability and API management
App Store and Play Store policy changes
Cost and development timelines

Which technologies, frameworks, or best practices are helping you overcome these challenges in 2026?

Looking forward to insights from experienced mobile developers and industry experts.

entry.title: Quality Inspection with POSC in SAP EWM
entry.content:
In SAP EWM, POSC is used to map complex processes and execute for processes. In this blog, We shall cover basic POSC scenario with Quality inspection in SAP EWM.  Please note that Quality management being used in this case is for Embedded EWM. Post reading of this blog, you should be able to understand:
1. POSC config and working
2. All required configurations for Quality Inspection in embedded EWM.

We will first start with POSC settings.

Expected flow:

  1. Movement of HUs from door to staging area
  2. Movement from staging area to QC area

Decision taking using QM

  1. Putaway to storage type, as different stock types based on quality decision. (Blocked or unrestricted)

Configurations required:

Assuming we already have a basic warehouse setup- with document types etc, Let’s start with POSC:

Assignment of External steps:

External steps are what control or where we define each steps to be executed in POSC. The external steps are assigned as steps and linked to a storage process. When  Here we will define 3 steps: Unload, Quality inspection and putaway:

Assignment of storage process:

A storage process is to be defined and the steps IB01, QIS, IB03 assigned under it.

Definition of storage types, Bins for different areas:
Storage type & bin for Door:

GI area:

Putaway area:

Defining WPT:

Lets start with WPT for moving HU from Door to our GR Zone:

Storage process is linked to the WPT and we have enabled the “confirmed immediately” to ensure it’s a one-step unloading.

WPT for movement from GR Zone to Quality inspection area:

Again, we link our storage process, and we provide the source storage type and bin here, which is the GR Zone. Destination are controlled via POSC for PTWY activity.

WPT for putaway from QI area to Main Zone:

1010 will be our main WPT which would perform the final putaway activity. For POSC, We define the initial source location for the WPT. 

1010 will be defined as the main WPT for goods movement under the Inbound delivery document.

WPT determination for inbound delivery:

Staging area and door determination:

Assumption: You have already defined door, staging bay etc

System will pick the below warehouse door and bay for the 1010 goods movement WPT.

Putaway strategy:

Create Storage type search sequence:

Assigning storage types to STSS:

Defining PACI and assigning it to STSS:

This same PACI will be linked to the material under Whse data tab:

Search sequence for putaway:

POSC:

Unloading step-IB01, HU travels from Door to the GR ZONE

Quality inspection step- QIS- Moves from GR ZONE to Quality inspection area Putaway will be taken care by putaway strategy we maintained above.

Quality Management settings:

Activate Business Transaction Event QBEXT (QM) :

User transaction: FIBF

Settings → Identification → SAP Applications. Verify that Application ID QBEXT is active

Quality work center:

SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Master Data → Work Center → Specify Work Center Layout

Assign Default Work Center for Inspection:

SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management →

Cross-Process Settings → Quality Management → Inspection Results → Assign Default Work Center for Inspections

Keep in mind that we have already defined the POSC step for QI step

Enabling application log:

/n/SCWM/ACTLOG

Warehouse dependant IOTs:

SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management →

Cross-Process Settings → Quality Management → Basics and Integration → Define and Activate WarehouseDependent IOTs

 

Follow on actions for inspection results:

SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management →

Cross-Process Settings → Quality Management → Inspection Results → Maintain Follow-Up Actions

EDIT CODE GROUPS AND CODES FOR USAGE DECISIONS (QM):

QM Node> Quality Inspection → Inspection Lot Completion → Edit Code Groups and Codes for Usage Decisions

Assign codes for usage decision for Code group

 

EDIT SELECTED SETS FOR USAGE DECISION (QM):

Quality Management → Quality Inspection → Inspection Lot Completion → Edit Selected Sets for Usage Decisions

To define the inspection types and the controls associated with the inspection type. The inspection type determines how an inspection is to be carried out.:

Quality Management → Quality Inspection → Inspection Lot Creation → Maintain Inspection Types

Define default values for a material's inspection setup:

Quality Management → Quality Inspection → Inspection Lot Creation → Define Default Values for Inspection Type

MAINTAIN SETTINGS AT PLANT LEVEL:

Quality Management → Basic Settings → Maintain Settings at Plant Level Define your plant and assign the inspection type specific settings:

ASSIGN LOGISTICAL FOLLOW-UP ACTIONS (EWM):

SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management →

Cross-Process Settings → Quality Management → Inspection Results → Maintain Follow-Up

Maintain similar entry for EWM_AP:

In above screenshots, We have linked follow up actions with stock type, WPT details.

Cut over Activity: You have maintained and released an inspection plan in app (transaction QP01) for your material and plant. 

The material we use here is “57”, Usage would be GR and overall status would be “released”

Material, QM tab:

Inspection set-up: Click on the “insp. Setup” button to enable the inspection for this particular material.

For simple QM process, we are activating “01” which is for Goods Receipt Insp. for Purchase Order.

Testing:

Creation on PO and distribution of delivery to EWM:

As you see, Goods movement WPT is determined properly, Also Stock type is Q3, which is stock in QI putaway.

Product is packed:

Click on unloading:

As you see, movement has taken place from Door to GR Zone using WPT Y365:

Confirming the open WT from GR Zone for QI Area:

Taking Quality decision:

Get quality inspection document from Item tab from inbound delivery item. QI document gets generated during GR.

Tasks created:

The highlighted task is performing a posting change from Quality to Unrestricted stock type, and another follow on task is created to the main putaway area using 1010 WPT.

You can also view entire details for the inspection document in monitor:

Monitor> Documents> WT

Delivery document updated with putaway status:

Now, Taking decision for rejection:

Product has stock type changed to “blocked” and has and moved to specific area BLC-01 Please note that we have defined separate storage type sequence for the QI rejection area

With this, we have completed and tested set up for Quality management with usage decision (with different strategies)using POSC. You can map several kind of processes like Unloading, Any value added services etc by using POSC. Please let me know your thoughts!


Thanks,

Aman

entry.title: Issue with S4 app - Schedule Order Acknowledgement output for Purchase Orders
entry.content:
Hello Team, We are currently testing the S/4HANA app “Schedule Order Acknowledgement Output” for Purchase Orders, specifically for the Order Acknowledgement Reminder scenario.

As per the Confirmation Control configuration, the setup is as follows:

  • Monitoring period: 2 days
  • Reference date: PO date + monitoring time
  • “Subject to Reminder” checkbox: Enabled

Based on this configuration, we need to understand the application behavior for the following points:

  1. Reminder Triggering Logic
    We expect to receive reminders until the AB Order Acknowledgement confirmation is received from the supplier. However, we observe inconsistent behavior:

    • For some POs, reminders are not received at all
    • For other POs, reminders are received daily

  2. PO Selection Criteria
    We would like to understand how the app selects Purchase Orders for sending reminders.

  3. Reminder Stop Condition
    Our understanding is that reminders should stop only after the AB Order Acknowledgement confirmation is received from the supplier.
    Please clarify:

    • Is this the only stop condition?
    • Is there a way to restrict or stop reminders when no AB Order Acknowledgement is received from the supplier over a certain period?

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Issue with S4 app - Schedule Order Acknowledgement ou...
entry.content:
Hi,

Based on SAP standard behavior, the reminder job selects only those Purchase Orders that fulfill all prerequisite conditions, including:

  • Confirmation Control Key configured for AB acknowledgement
  • “Subject to Reminder” enabled
  • No Order Acknowledgement confirmation received yet
  • Initial PO output successfully generated
  • Monitoring period reached
  • PO item remains relevant for processing

This explains the observed behavior where:

  • Some POs are skipped because one or more eligibility conditions are not fulfilled
  • Some POs receive reminders repeatedly because the background job continues to run and no AB confirmation has been received

Regarding the stop condition:

  • Standard SAP behavior stops reminders once the supplier Order Acknowledgement (AB confirmation) is received
  • Reminders may also stop if the PO item becomes no longer relevant (completed/deleted/final delivered)

Currently, SAP standard does not provide functionality to:

  • Limit the number of reminders
  • Automatically stop reminders after a defined period if no supplier response is received

Therefore, if the job is scheduled daily, reminders can also be generated daily by design.

We also reviewed the following SAP documentation/KBA which is relevant for this scenario:

  • How to generate outputs for purchase order dunning reminders?
  • How to generate outputs for reminders of purchase order acknowledgements?
  • Schedule Dunning Reminder Output for Purchase Orders
  • Schedule Order Acknowledgement Output for Purchase Orders

Refer to

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003385359

Recommended actions:

  • Review the job logs for skipped Purchase Orders
  • Verify output status and confirmation control configuration for affected POs
  • Adjust job recurrence frequency if daily reminders are not desired
  • Use the “Monitor Receipt of Order Acknowledgments” app for tracking supplier confirmations

If business requires additional logic such as:

  • Maximum reminder count
  • Escalation handling
  • Automatic stop after X days

this would require an enhancement request or custom extension.

Thanks and regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Must-See Utilities Highlights at SAP Sapphire Madrid
entry.content:
Dear Utilities Community,

SAP Sapphire kicked off last week in Orlando where SAP CEO Christian Klein announced the Business AI Platform as the foundation for the Autonomous Enterprise,  enabling AI agents to run end‑to‑end business processes with deep business context, governance, and security. SAP’s vision goes beyond traditional ERP by embedding AI directly into mission‑critical workflows, helping organizations turn AI investments into tangible, accurate, and scalable business outcomes.

Explore the SAP Sapphire 2026 news and innovations and watch keynotes on demand. 

 

This week, SAP Sapphire continues in Madrid. While the core content remains similar, discussions often take on a more European perspective, with a stronger focus on regulation, regional market dynamics, and local customer success stories.
 
There are many exciting sessions—I'd especially like to highlight three Utilities-specific highlights, all taking place on Wednesday, May 20.

Ignite the future: Accelerate your energy transition | IND1261 at 10:30 CEST in Hall 9, Industries Center, Theater 

The global race toward energy transition presents not only complex challenges but also unprecedented opportunities for industry leaders and consumers. SAP’s groundbreaking innovations, real-world AI applications, and fresh partnerships are reshaping the landscape. Thrive in a rapidly evolving market and be at the forefront of the energy revolution. Join Filip Kowalski's (MEE Industry Lead for Utilities and Oil, Gas & Energy) presentation. 

National Grid transforms capital investment accounting with SAP BTP and AI | BTP1483 at 1:00 PM CEST in Hall 10, Theater 10

Discover how agentic AI is enabling the next generation of capital investment accounting with SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and AI. Hear how AI-powered agents at British energy company National Grid plc help autonomously orchestrate processes, improve financial transparency, and support faster, more-informed investment decisions. The session will be presented by Aley Matthewson, SAP Functional Manager at National Grid and Blake Goddard, Senior Business AI Platfrom Executive at SAP. 

RWE: AI-driven offshore wind troubleshooting | IND1609 at 3:30 PM CEST in Hall 10, Theater 14 

RWE uses AI in SAP solutions to speed offshore wind repairs and boost asset uptime. Technicians deploy AI-assisted SAP solutions to analyze past notes and OEM manuals, pinpoint root causes, and draft tailored repair work orders. Field technicians get support to improve repair documentation and feed insights into the system for continuous learning.

Join the session from Frank Scholtka, Director Operations UK at RWE Offshore Wind GmbH and Bruno Pincolini, Global Lead for SAP Business AI for Utilities at SAP.

 
Join us also at the Utilities Industry Expert Station in Hall 9 (Industries Center – IND1520) to connect 1:1 with SAP specialists and explore your key challenges and opportunities. Discover how AI is reshaping industry processes at the Experience Hub (EXP1536), and gain valuable insights into SAP’s portfolio during the Utilities & Oil & Gas Industry Tour. Reach out to your SAP Account Manager to reserve your spot and make the most of these tailored experiences.

Visit the SAP Sapphire Madrid website to explore topics and build your personal schedule. 

I am looking forward to hearing your feedback.

Best regards,

Nicole

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #2 — Event-Driven Utilities: Designing Real-Time Flows on SAP BTP
entry.content:
ATUL Architecture #2 — Event-Driven Utilities: Designing Real-Time Flows on SAP BTP

Introduction

Most of the utilities organization did not build batch-driven systems because they were wrong. They built them because, for many years, batch processing was enough and serving the business need to generate the mass customer bills and satisfying other business processes like payment processing, return and refunds etc.

Meter reads come daily. Billing jobs ran overnight. Outage analysis could wait a few minutes. But now with the new AI Era this world is changing fast.

That’s means today, utilities operate in an environment where:

  • Smart meters generate continuous streams of events.
  • EV charging creates unpredictable load spikes,
  • Distributed energy resources change grid behavior dynamically,
  • Customers expect instant updates and near real-time visibility,

In this environment, delays are no longer just technical limitations — they directly impact operations and customer experience.

Let’s understand this with a simple example occurs daily.

A customer loses power at 2:05 PM, the smart meter detects the outage immediately.

But the utility system processes that information at 2:30 PM, the question is why and the answer is because the event is sitting inside a scheduled batch cycle. Now that 25-minute delay creates:

  • Slower outage response.
  • Increased call center traffic.
  • Poor customer experience.
  • Potential regulatory exposure.

This is where event-driven architecture becomes critical. And this is where SAP BTP can play a major role in modern utility architecture.

This is exactly where event-driven architecture comes in—and where SAP BTP starts to make a real difference.

1.       Why Event-Driven Utilities Matter (In Real Life)

Utilities are no longer operating predictable, centralized grids.

The grid is becoming:

  • Distributed
  • Dynamic
  • High-volume
  • Event-heavy

Millions of devices are continuously generating signals:

  • Smart meters
  • Grid sensors
  • DER systems
  • EV charging stations
  • Field devices

Traditional batch systems struggle in this environment — not because the technology is bad, but because the operating model has changed.

Event-driven architecture changes the model completely.

That’s means Instead of waiting for scheduled processing windows or batch, systems react when events occur. In simple terms real time processing windows.

That enables:

  • Faster outage detection
  • Near real-time billing updates
  • Automated operational workflows
  • Immediate exception handling
  • Better grid visibility

This shift is not only about speed. It is about building utilities that can respond continuously instead of periodically.

2.       Where SAP BTP Fits In (Simple View)

Think of SAP BTP Event Mesh as the traffic controller for events. In many utilities, integration landscapes become tightly coupled over time.

One system talks directly to another.  Then another connection gets added. Then another.

Eventually, even small changes become difficult and risky.

This is where SAP Event Mesh on SAP BTP becomes important.

Think of Event Mesh as the event backbone of the enterprise.

Instead of systems communicating directly:

  • Systems publish events
  • Interested systems subscribe to them
  • Processing becomes decoupled and scalable

In a utility environment:

  • AMI systems publish meter events
  • Grid applications publish operational events
  • SAP S/4HANA consumes only business-relevant events
  • Other BTP services enrich, filter, or analyze the data

This creates flexibility that traditional point-to-point integration cannot provide.

But there is an important architectural reality here:

Poor event design creates the same problems as poor API design.

If event topics, queue structures, and filtering rules are not designed carefully, Event Mesh can quickly become another bottleneck.

3.       A Simple End-to-End Example (AMI → BTP → S/4HANA)

Let’s walk through a simple but real-time business case or scenario.

Scenario of Power outage detected

  • Step 1 — Meter detects outage: A smart meter immediately detects loss of power.
  • Smart meter sends an outage event immediately.
  • This goes to SAP BTP (via MQTT or API).
  • At this point, no batch job is waiting to process the information later. The event is available immediately.

 

  • Step 2 — SAP BTP Processes the Event

From there:

  • Integration services validate the payload.
  • Business rules evaluate the event.
  • Additional context is added.

For example:

  • Is this a single-home outage?
  • Or are multiple meters reporting outages from the same feeder?

This is where intelligence starts to matter. BTP services can:

  • Correlate events,
  • Filter noise
  • Detect patterns
  • Trigger automation

In many architectures, this becomes the real operational brain of the utility platform.

  • Step 3 — SAP S/4HANA Executes the Business Process

 Once the event is validated:

  • A work order can be created automatically
  • Field crews can be dispatched
  • Customers can receive notifications
  • Device management processes can begin
  • Billing or service workflows can update

S/4HANA remains the system of record. But SAP BTP handles the real-time orchestration layer around it. That separation is important for scalability.

4.        What Actually Changes (Batch vs Real-Time)

Traditional Batch Model

  • Meter data processed hourly or daily
  • Exceptions reviewed manually
  • Outage visibility delayed
  • Billing corrections handled later
  • Integrations tightly coupled

Event-Driven Model

  • Continuous event processing
  • Automated workflow triggering
  • Near real-time outage detection
  • Dynamic operational response
  • Scalable publish/subscribe integrations

But there is an important operational truth many projects have discovered late:

Real-time systems expose process problems that batch systems quietly hide.

When systems react immediately:

  • Data quality issues surface faster, and this is real point of having BTP.
  • Business rule conflicts become visible, immediate effect/result.
  • Operational inefficiencies can no longer stay hidden behind overnight processing.

This is why event-driven transformation is both technical and organizational.

5.       Where Things Usually Go Wrong (From Experience)

Mostly in real projects, challenges show up quickly:

  • Event storms: Millions of AMI events can flood the platform during outages or restoration cycles. Without proper throttling and filtering, downstream systems become overwhelmed.
  • Poor event design: If event topics are designed inconsistently:
    • Consumers become tightly coupled
    • Governance becomes difficult
    • Reusability disappears
  • Overloading S/4HANA: Not every event belongs inside ERP. One of the biggest mistakes is forwarding raw operational noise directly into S/4HANA. ERP should receive business-relevant outcomes — not every technical signal.
  • Duplicate processing: In distributed event systems, duplicate events are normal. Architectures must be designed with idempotency in mind from the beginning.

And the biggest one:

Business rules have become more complex than expected. In most implementations, BTP becomes the brain, not just the pipe.

6.        Architecture Principle That Matters Most

The biggest mindset about this modern architecture shift is this:

SAP BTP should not behave like traditional middleware.

And it should be important to understand since most of the client leadership want new system but same like old. It should behave like:

  • An orchestration platform
  • An event intelligence layer
  • A real-time decision platform

That is a very different architectural role.

7.       Key Patterns That Actually Work

From a practitioner point of view, these patterns matter:

  • Use publish/subscribe, not point-to-point integrations
  • Filter and enrich events before they reach S/4HANA
  • Keep S/4HANA focused on business transactions, not raw events
  • Design for idempotency (avoid duplicates)
  • Design events for reuse and scalability
  • Treat Event Mesh as a platform capability, not just middleware

8.       Final Thought

Important things we need to understand as architects and as organization leaders, moving to event-driven architecture is not simply a technology modernization effort. It will change how utilities operate, most of the way business processes execute may change or enhance. That’s result an organization becoming:

  • Faster decisions which will improve business process.
  • Better customer experience.
  • More automation, less manual work.
  • Less dependency on batch cycles.

But it also requires discipline in design. Because one system reacts instantly:


Its means There is very little room for poorly designed processes, noisy integrations, or unclear ownership.

In real-time utilities, architecture quality becomes operational quality.

Next in the Series

ATUL Architecture #3

In the next article, we move closer to the operational grid layer:

  • OT/IT convergence
  • MQTT ingestion patterns
  • IEC 61850 integration
  • Substation-to-cloud flows
  • Security and isolation models.

This is where utility architecture starts moving from enterprise latency… to grid latency.
